words: This conversation with WILLIAM STOWELL, who is known by most people as,
"Michigan Bill", took place at his home in Lewiston, Idaho on October 29,
1975. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER. (There is some technical difficul
ty with this tape.)
WILLIAM STOWELL: I come from Canada, but I worked all over upper Michigan.
I was born in Canada.
SAM SCHRAGER: I've heard that upper peninsula there in Michigan was some log
ging country.

speaker: SS
words: How did you wind up in Michigan?

speaker: WS
words: In Michigan?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, how did you wind up there?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, just tramping around,I job, you know. Finally wound up

speaker: WS
words: there. Spent a few years there, not too long. Maybe six, seven years,

speaker: SS
words: When?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, in the early twenties. Twenty, twenty-one, twenty-two.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard something about that country. Were there a lot of Finns?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, Christ, yes. That, was all Fins, that mining country. That was

speaker: WS
words: copper. Yeah, that was ninety percent Finns. Yeah. I knew some of

speaker: WS
words: 'em. I didn't never work in the mines, though. I was arouild 'em a

speaker: WS
words: lot. They worked in the woods, too. Yeah, there was lots of Finns

speaker: WS
words: there.

speaker: SS
words: Did you come out here from there?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Was this country very much like that as far as the what the logging

speaker: SS
words: life was like? Was it similar?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, practically the same when I came here, except it was steeper

speaker: WS
words: ground, you know. There it was all level. But here it is pretty

speaker: WS
words: hilly, steep. Its a little different logging, you know. Of course,

speaker: WS
words: they used horses both places But they used dogs in this country,

speaker: WS
words: you know. Nailed together, you see. And long trails of, well,

speaker: WS
words: twenty, twenty-five at the most, you know, logs. And then chutes

speaker: WS
words: and flumes. They'd chute 'em to the flume and then flume 'em to the

speaker: WS
words: river. That's the way they done it here. Too steep for railroads.

speaker: WS
words: There was no trucks at that time, so, that's the way they did it.

speaker: WS
words: You know a chute?

speaker: SS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: WS
words: Well, that's the way they went.

speaker: SS
words: Was it the same thing for the loggers? Same kind of guys out here

speaker: SS
words: as there was back there?

speaker: WS
words: Well, there was more foreigners back there than here. But, other

speaker: WS
words: wise it was the same. Camps, you know. Conditions were different here,

speaker: WS
words: they were much better, much better.

speaker: SS
words: Were they better here?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, Lord, God, yes. Yeah. Well, they eliminated the to^p bunkswell,

speaker: WS
words: that was later years that they done that, but there was double

speaker: WS
words: deck bunks. Two in a bunk. Uppers and lowers.^ And that is bad bus

speaker: WS
words: iness. That is one reason I got out of there. Not much chance to

speaker: WS
words: improve it. But this country here was a way different.A Clean linen,

speaker: WS
words: you know, every week for your bed. It got to be later years all bot

speaker: WS
words: tom bunks, you know. They was no top. Oh, yes, much better here.

speaker: SS
words: Did you hear much about the the work they did to improve the

speaker: SS
words: conditions?

speaker: WS
words: Yes, Yes, they accomplished wonders.. Yeah. They did. They was

speaker: WS
words: kind of a rough and tough outfit, but they got the work done. They

speaker: WS
words: got that job done. Direct action is what it was. There was no po

speaker: WS
words: litical harum-scarum mixed up in it. It was all direct, on the job.

speaker: WS
words: That's how it worked. Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: About what year was it, you got here? In Idaho. Came to Idaho?

speaker: SS
words: By that time, now, the way I've heard it, is was about back in 1917

speaker: SS
words: or '18 that the IWff s cleaned up the camps.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yes, yes. And up until '23— the last big strike was in '23—

speaker: WS
words: general strike, for ten days. That was all. It was for the prisoners

speaker: WS
words: that was in jail, see. Those that were put in jail during these strikes.

speaker: SS
words: During the earlier ones?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Was it to get them out?

speaker: WS
words: No. no. They didn't make it but they shut everything down, see, that's

speaker: WS
words: all it was. They couldn't get it. But they did make a little up

speaker: WS
words: rising here in '36, again, if you remember that. Don't you?

speaker: SS
words: I've heard that.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, that was for conditions, mostly, and wages. Well, they wanted

speaker: WS
words: a minimum of five dollars a day. And clean out the camps. The camps

speaker: WS
words: were getting pretty scroungy, you know. I don't know whether it was

speaker: WS
words: won or lost. We were out a couple of months. I was working for the

speaker: WS
words: Potlatch, then, but we were out a couple of months. I don't believe

speaker: WS
words: we lost anything. They cleaned the camps up. And raised the wages.

speaker: WS
words: But they didn't do that til after the strike, you see. They broke

speaker: WS
words: the strike, that's what happened.

speaker: SS
words: Well, you know that's the story that I've heard before. That way the

speaker: SS
words: company says that it wasn't the IWW's that mad because they

speaker: SS
words: ended the strike and then they made But it's all one

speaker: SS
words: and the same thing.

speaker: WS
words: Yes, I look it that way. They didn't do it til after the strike,

speaker: WS
words: but they did it. But that was the last feeble attempt of the IWW's

speaker: WS
words: that they ever made, in this country.

speaker: SS
words: I was surprised when I heard about the '36 stike that they were still

speaker: SS
words: kicking around all that time. Because these others,the AF of L and

speaker: SS
words: those * were getting in and the CIO, too.

speaker: WS
words: Yes. Well, the CIO wasn't too husky yet, in this country, you know.

speaker: WS
words: They come in afterwards. The AF of L got booted out and the CIO took

speaker: WS
words: over by a large margin, you know. But, I don't know the AF of L, I

speaker: WS
words: guess they were— Well, the CIO was political, too, but the other

speaker: WS
words: was moreso. I belonged to the CIO, but I never did belong to the

speaker: WS
words: AF of L in the woods. I belonged to the piledrivers and the like of

speaker: WS
words: that.

speaker: SS
words: I had the idea that the AFof L was more like a craft union than it

speaker: SS
words: was a real industrial union.

speaker: WS
words: Sure, it was, that's what it was, yes. It was a craft union.

speaker: SS
words: That way it'd make more sense. To me the CIO would be in the lum

speaker: SS
words: ber industry.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, yeah. It was the lesser evil of the two, but I don't know, I—

speaker: WS
words: beings that I had belonged to the IWW, I couldn't see much to either

speaker: WS
words: one of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think made the IWW's so much a better union than the CIO?

speaker: WS
words: Well, you could act, more or less, without all these executive boards

speaker: WS
words: and the like of that, you know. You could hold a meeting and youCoul*

speaker: WS
words: decide to do something, and majority ruled. Take a vote, and if

speaker: WS
words: the majority wanted it, why, that's the way it was. It went, as far

speaker: WS
words: as the crew was concerned. We didn't always win, but it was an ef

speaker: WS
words: fort, anyway. So, that was what I liked about it. You got an organ

speaker: WS
words: ization, there's too much politcal. High salaries. Big shots. All

speaker: WS
words: that. It don't work with the common stiff, you know. I don't fig

speaker: WS
words: ure it does.

speaker: SS
words: Most of these unions it never gets down to the workingman. I mean,

speaker: SS
words: he never really has any say-so about it. Those decisions are made

speaker: SS
words: up there and—

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. Yeah. That's right.

speaker: SS
words: Were you ever in camps that you guys took a vote and then went out

speaker: SS
words: on strike?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of reason would make you decide you had to do that?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, well, different reasons. Mostly— especially the IWW's. They

speaker: WS
words: were mostly for conditions. Working conditions and living oonditions.

speaker: WS
words: Get away from them old double bunks and lousy camps and bum chuck

speaker: WS
words: and all that. They were trying to get something better— well, bet

speaker: WS
words: ter living. Is what it was, really. They never did strike too much

speaker: WS
words: for money, because they take that away from you anyway. That money.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, I don't— I think there are IWW's never come back, you know.

speaker: WS
words: The IWW, they're done. They were revolutionary, you know. And a re-

speaker: WS
words: volutionary organization at this day and age don't get along very

speaker: WS
words: good.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think it made more sense in the off days than it would make

speaker: SS
words: nowadays?

speaker: WS
words: Well, that's kind of a hard question to answer. I wouldn't attempt

speaker: WS
words: to answer it. I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: Conditions have changed a lot. I sometimes wonder, whether it's

speaker: SS
words: changed all that much, or whether—

speaker: WS
words: People change, you know. People have changed an awful lot. Oh yes.

speaker: WS
words: That's what makes it, I think. Is the people, themselves. You see

speaker: WS
words: the lumberjack was always a single man, a footloose man, you know.

speaker: WS
words: He didn't give a Goddam for anything. He had nothing to lose. And

speaker: WS
words: the world to gain. So that's what made him that way, I believe.

speaker: WS
words: I'm pretty sure that's the way it was with me. Say a hundred and

speaker: WS
words: fifty men in a camp, they might be two percent married, or maybe one

speaker: WS
words: percent. All single. And they had no responsibilities except them

speaker: WS
words: selves. Anything they could gain, was for them. Now, you see, they're

speaker: WS
words: all married. They got responsiblities. Payments on cars and homes

speaker: WS
words: and TVs and whatnot. Those days, all a man had was his packsack.

speaker: WS
words: Just what was on his back, that's all.

speaker: SS
words: It seems so different than it is, as you were saying then and now.

speaker: SS
words: I can't even think of how the situation would arise, so many men

speaker: SS
words: would be single and not be tied down by anything.

speaker: WS
words: It's hard to figure that out, isn't it?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: WS
words: I don't know why they never got married. I can't figure that out.

speaker: WS
words: Well, I never did. I was just from job to job, you know, and I didn't

speaker: WS
words: have no responsibility. Couldn't afford to have many, unless I

speaker: WS
words: wanted to settle down and take less money for some other job, you know.

speaker: WS
words: We always made a little more than the town workers.

speaker: SS
words: Why was that? You guys could go anyplace where there was a job?

speaker: WS
words: Where the money was, yefrk. Well, then the gyppo system come in. Of

speaker: WS
words: course that changed everything. There was a minimum wage but it was

speaker: WS
words: awful small. Everybody wants to gyppo, gyppo, gyppo. And they al

speaker: WS
words: ways made it so you could— well, you'd come out a third above the

speaker: WS
words: regular wages, anyway, you see, by going the limit, you know. Doing

speaker: WS
words: the utmost, your very best, every day, you had to. That's the way

speaker: WS
words: that was.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a good deal? I mean it sounds— they must have got a lot

speaker: SS
words: more work out of the men.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yes. Sure. No,that wasn't a good deal for us at all. No, we

speaker: WS
words: tried to abolish it, but you couldn't. There was too many that

speaker: WS
words: wanted it that way. You know, really wanted it that way. Right down

speaker: WS
words: in their hearts they wanted it that way. I mean, good militant union

speaker: WS
words: men, too, they were. But, if there was any trouble at all there were

speaker: WS
words: always ready to help out. Most of 'em wanted the gyppo system. Lit

speaker: WS
words: tle more money. You know, that's what drives people on, is that money.

speaker: SS
words: Sure, for a few years a guy can break his back working if he's young,

speaker: SS
words: and really can put out the work, but seems like it's gonna catch up

speaker: SS
words: with 'em after while.

speaker: WS
words: Well, it does. Naturally it does, sure. You may think you can do

speaker: WS
words: it, but you can't. You'll poop out pretty Goddam sudden like when

speaker: WS
words: you get older. That's awful hard work in them olden days, you know.

speaker: WS
words: Everything was handwork. The sawing was all handwork, bucking was

speaker: WS
words: all handwork, and the skidding was, oh, well, the horses, you know,

speaker: WS
words: but peavy work, you know, you had all that to do* and that run into

speaker: WS
words: work.

speaker: SS
words: Did the IWW have on that? Did they say gyppo was no good?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. That's the stand they took. Absolutely no good, no.

speaker: SS
words: If you really believed in the union, then the gyppo would be very

speaker: SS
words: unequal, because the guys that could put out the work could make a

speaker: SS
words: lot better than the old-timers, who couldn't do it. Is that right?

speaker: WS
words: Yes, but when you got old enough, you quit gyppoing, though, you

speaker: WS
words: couldn't. You had to quit. (Chuckles) That was the way that worked.

speaker: WS
words: Well, you were good up til fifty years old, you know. You knew a

speaker: WS
words: lot of shortcuts, by that time, that you didn't know when you were

speaker: WS
words: young er.

speaker: SS
words: What?

speaker: WS
words: Well, just generally speaking, you know.

speaker: SS
words: The way to do the work — you mean, like you learned, you really

speaker: SS
words: mastered the whole job.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, sure, that's it. Yeah, that's my idea, yes. It's just as I

speaker: WS
words: say, you learn a lot of tricks and shortcuts. It all help out at the

speaker: WS
words: end of the day, you know. You have accomplished a little more than

speaker: WS
words: than the absolute greenhorns would, and done it just about as easy.

speaker: SS
words: I heard that most of the early lumberjacks that were out in this

speaker: SS
words: country were old men. Older men. Is that true? As far as you knew,

speaker: SS
words: in the early days?

speaker: WS
words: There were a lot of old men, yes. They were a lot of 'em pretty old

speaker: WS
words: when they come to this country, you know, from the East. They were

speaker: WS
words: all from the East, practically. Eastern Canada and the Midwestern

speaker: WS
words: states, Wisconsin, Minnesota and Michigan. That's where most of 'em

speaker: WS
words: were from when I came out here. And they were all men along in iheir

speaker: WS
words: years,

speaker: SS
words: You were one of the young ones?

speaker: WS
words: Yes. Yes, sure. I was only about twenty-three, I think, when I

speaker: WS
words: landed here. But, all I'd ever did was work in the wood?, you know,

speaker: WS
words: when I was a kid at home. Well, the Old Man had timber, you know.

speaker: WS
words: The old sawmill.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, old waterpower sawmill.

speaker: SS
words: You must have seen it ever since you were old enough to see things.

speaker: WS
words: Yes, sure.

speaker: SS
words: Did he let you work on it?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, no. No, I just was around it, you know. I never done nothing

speaker: WS
words: around it. Piled a little lumber, something like that. Worked

speaker: WS
words: on the pond a little bit. I was going to school then. I didn't do

speaker: WS
words: much work.

speaker: SS
words: Where was that in Canada?

speaker: WS
words: In Quebec; the Province of Quebec. Little small town about the size

speaker: WS
words: of Pierce, I guess.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, no, he had a sawyer, that's all. The neighbors, they helped out.

speaker: WS
words: Do a little work for a little lumber. Work a day or two and get some

speaker: WS
words: lumb er.

speaker: SS
words: Did you come down to the States because there was more work here?

speaker: WS
words: No. No, I come down to go to school.

speaker: SS
words: Oh!

speaker: WS
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: You were still just a kid?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah. I was about seven, I guess.

speaker: WS
words: You know in the Midwest— those lumber towns were really tough towns.

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yeah. Of course, they had calmed down some, when I got there.

speaker: WS
words: They used to fight for the fun of it. But, I don't know, they kinda

speaker: WS
words: cut that out. People change, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Fights were just over — when drinking and that.

speaker: WS
words: Well, yes. There'd be one tough guy and the other A think he was

speaker: WS
words: tougher, and he'd maybe go over to the camp where the other guy was

speaker: WS
words: working just to challenge him. That's the way that worked. And

speaker: WS
words: they were rough, too. Lot of 'em done all their fighting on the

speaker: WS
words: floor, they'd chew and bitin', and if they were on their feet they

speaker: WS
words: were usin' their shoes.

speaker: SS
words: Cork boots?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, they went in to win, regardless.

speaker: SS
words: Did any grudges go on for a very long time?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, no. They were forgotten. Sometimes they weren't, but most of

speaker: WS
words: the time they were. Have it out and over with and go back to drink

speaker: WS
words: ing.

speaker: SS
words: Well, was that the same out here, too? Were the towns pretty tough?

speaker: SS
words: Out here, the logging towns?

speaker: WS
words: Some of 'em were, yes. Like Aberdeen and Hoquiam. Aberdeen was a

speaker: WS
words: rough town. Washington. That was about the roughest on the West

speaker: WS
words: Coast, I think. Well,they was longshoremen there and lumberjacks

speaker: WS
words: and everything, you know. Timber was close to town. And that made

speaker: WS
words: a difference, and it was still the single men that was working then.

speaker: SS
words: Did you usually stay with a job for very long?

speaker: WS
words: Me?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, I have stayed six to eight months. And I've stayed two days.

speaker: WS
words: Varied, it depended on how the job was, or something. I usually

speaker: WS
words: stayed pretty good. Well, it all depends on how things go. If it

speaker: WS
words: suited you, you'd stay, if it didn't, you'd go.

speaker: SS
words: The different bosses, you know, formen, did that make a big differ-

speaker: SS
words: ence?

speaker: WS
words: Well, it would, yeah. Sometimes. But some people— with me

speaker: WS
words: it didn't make any difference who I worked for. I could work for

speaker: WS
words: anybody; long as I get along with 'em. But never inter

speaker: WS
words: fered with you much. If you were gyppoing you wouldn't see 'em,

speaker: WS
words: maybe, from the time you started until you quit. You'd see 'em in

speaker: WS
words: camp, you know, but you wouldn't see 'em on the job. But if you

speaker: WS
words: were working by the day, you'd see 'em. They'd be around then ev-

speaker: WS
words: ery day But they didn't bother you much. No.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think there was much difference between them? Were some

speaker: SS
words: foremen really working for the company and others working more for

speaker: SS
words: the men?

speaker: SS
words: Well, you can't be both ways. You couldn't be both ways. Some

speaker: SS
words: lean a little bit towards the crew, if they come up from the crew,

speaker: SS
words: you know what I mean. If they were picked out of the crew, or some

speaker: SS
words: thing like that. They would lean a little towards their fellowman,

speaker: SS
words: but, ordinarily, of course, he was working for the company. But, you

speaker: SS
words: can't hold that againsit him.' And in order to hold the job, they had

speaker: SS
words: to be. That was my idea, always. Generally speaking, they were pret

speaker: SS
words: ty good bunch of guys.I never seen any of 'em that weren't, anyway.

speaker: SS
words: Axel Anderson, do you know him?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yeah. I worked for him. He's living yet. In Spokane,

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, I talked to him up there. Oh, I talked to him for hours and

speaker: SS
words: hours.

speaker: SS
words: Gosh, he must be old.

speaker: SS
words: About ninety-one, maybe.

speaker: SS
words: And did you know Maler, his brother?

speaker: SS
words: No, I don't know him. I've heard of him.

speaker: SS
words: He was a good guy. Real good guy. Axel's alright too, but, Malker

speaker: SS
words: was a really good fellow.

speaker: SS
words: He was a foreman, too, wasn't he?

speaker: SS
words: Yes. Good one.

speaker: SS
words: What would make a good foremah like him? What did he do, that made

speaker: SS
words: him a good foreman?

speaker: SS
words: Well, I don't know. He mixed with the crew, in town. on the job.

speaker: SS
words: And he drank with 'em. Just generally speaking, he got along real

speaker: SS
words: good with 'em. He give 'em a good price for their work, and he got

speaker: SS
words: a lot of work done. That was the whole sum and substance of that deal.

speaker: SS
words: Axel said that he thinks the push was in a mind, between the

speaker: SS
words: company and men. He said he used to like to try to do good by the

speaker: SS
words: men and every once in a while he'd get on the carpet with the company.

speaker: WS
words: Sure, sure. I imagine they did. I know they did. I never was a

speaker: WS
words: push, but— I kinda imagine that some of 'em would get called on the

speaker: WS
words: carpet for being a little bit too lenient, or lean a little bit towards

speaker: WS
words: the-^ you know. I know they do. Sure they do. Bound to.

speaker: SS
words: Was Malker a big man, like Axel?

speaker: WS
words: No. He was tall, but— a little taller than Axel, but he was slim

speaker: WS
words: mer. He was a little different man than Axel. I don't know why,

speaker: WS
words: but he was. Axel was pretty much company. But Malker, he didn't seem

speaker: WS
words: to lean that way too heavy, but he was a good push. He was goodreally-

speaker: WS
words: good for both sides. He got a lot of work done and he was

speaker: WS
words: good with the crew. I always liked him real well. I worked for the

speaker: WS
words: both of 'em. I used to work for Malker whenever I was on that side.

speaker: WS
words: See, this outfit here, what time did they consolidate? Tell you

speaker: WS
words: what—

speaker: SS
words: Something like that.

speaker: WS
words: I remember I was working up here at Camp H, and we got our September-

speaker: WS
words: October checks and they had a little strip of paper up in the cor

speaker: WS
words: ner— white strip of paper— They used to be the Clearwater Timber

speaker: WS
words: Company, you know. The cheks was made out that way. And there was a

speaker: WS
words: little piece of paper pasted onto the check— it said Potlatch For

speaker: WS
words: ests. I think that was in '31. I'm pretty sure it was. I know I

speaker: WS
words: was working.at Camp H for Old Brooks.

speaker: SS
words: Malker was on the other side?

speaker: WS
words: Yeah, he was always over there.

speaker: SS
words: Was there mu«h difference between the two sides? The camps and the

speaker: SS
words: men?

speaker: WS
words: Oh, yes. Yes. That other side always had better chuck, better camp

speaker: WS
words: conditions and everything. That's before they consolidated, you

speaker: WS
words: know. It was Potlatch Lumber Company.

speaker: SS
words: Right.

speaker: WS
words: That's what it was. And they had awful good horses. I used to like

speaker: WS
words: to go over there on account of the horses. They was good and they

speaker: WS
words: took good care of 'em. You couldn't abuse a team of horses there,

speaker: WS
words: you know. You wouldn't stay. Them pushers were all pretty much

speaker: WS

words: a saloon, he run that and he was making money hand over fist, but he

words: first one, but if you happened to come along right after that, you

words: was gone, too. I was working for him five months and never got canned.

words: I never did get canned. He never did can me. I,don't know why. If

words: I thought it was coming, Id quit, you see! (Chuckles) he was

words: day, stick around for a day or two." Or something like that, you

words: know. Yeah, he had a lot of good points. One of his boys is a contract

words: Weippe, I know that, i haven't seen him since.

words: Moscow.

words: Well, you ought to go up and see this profit. He's a nice guy to
